channel.title: Storytime on Hi everyone. It's me, Tim.
channel.description: Recent content in Storytime on Hi everyone. It's me, Tim.

item.title: Discovering D&D In 1979
item.description:
Hi everybody. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about discovering DnD in 1979.

Dungeons and Dragons was like nothing else that had ever been out before And I want to try to explain it to you. Because I was trying to explain it to a, coworker. Because if, you started playing DnD on a computer where there’s no DM, the computer handles it all. There are another players, There’s a tutorial that walks you through the basics so you have an idea of what to expect. You don’t have to learn the rules. I mean, you want to learn how things work, but you don’t have to learn how to run the rules.

item.title: Scenes From Unretirement
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about scenes from unretirement.

I did a video recently — and I’ll link it below — about me being unretired And it reminded me that over a year ago I did scenes from my life in game development where I showed a bunch of photos I had of and a few videos of stuff I had that happened to me while I was had a game development career Occurred to me. I’m back into game development. I should continue that video, so that’s what this is. I’m going to go away and just show you images and some videos and talk over them, and I’ll be back at the end, All righty. So let’s start with. I arrived back at Obsidian, here I am outside the offices. This is the first day I got back, October 30th 2025..

item.title: Unretirement
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about unretirement.

That’s what I’m calling my coming out of retirement, back into being employed, rather than calling it re-employed or unretire. Well, I guess I do call it a retirement, That’s what I call it.

Several people asked: what is that? What’s going on, What’s it like?

I’ll have to explain a bit more about my semi-retirement to make the unretirement make sense, but I do have a few videos on me being semi-retired and I’ll link them below so you can watch those. But, in short, what happened was when I moved to Seattle in 2020, Obsidian told me that they weren’t prepared to have outofstate employees. So I got switched to being a contractor. And I was fine. I was fine with that. This was before I had my YouTube channel, and it was right in the middle of COVID. This was June of 2020., so what happened was, for a few months, I don’t think anybody knew my status had changed.

item.title: Commenting On Comments
item.description:
Hi everybody. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to do some commenting on comments.

It’s a fun Friday, Something that’s been bubbling in my brain case and shows up in my notes. I mean I take notes about my channel. Why It’s me?

Of course I take notes. I write notes about comments, now this video is going to be similar to the other videos I have about making a channel. Um, I don’t even consider myself a YouTuber, but my Wikipedia page now says I’m a game developer and YouTuber. So it must be true.

item.title: The Joy Of Doing
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about the joy of doing.

I want to tell you a story today. Well, this, is going to go out as a fun Friday story. So it’s really just me telling a long story with a moral at the end, I guess [snorts]. But today is Monday, ironically, and this story just happened yesterday, which was a Sunday, which is kind of important to the story. But I’m digressing, I’m going to tell the story.

item.title: My Uncredited Game
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about my uncredited game.

I’ve mentioned it obliquely here and there that there’s a game out there that you know I developed that does not have my name in the credits by my choice. So it’s a fun Friday. I thought I’d talk about it, talk about why I had my name removed, and it will be a story. So let me explain everything that happened. So this is back in the ‘9s and a company had — I’m going to keep the company out of — this Company had a shipped game. So they had a game that they already designed, coded, tested and shipped. I had nothing to do with it and they had done two patches on it, one pretty extensive.

item.title: Deleted Channel Content
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to show you some deleted content from this channel.

I’ve been doing this channel for more than two years now And, while it’s unscripted and I don’t edit anything that goes up, there has been enough deleted content, consisting mostly of failed intros that I didn’t continue with some bloopers, just some deleted content that I didn’t want to go with. And I thought you know what. This stuff never made it onto the channel until now. So enjoy. Hi everyone, it’s me, Tim. Welcome to a Insomnia edition of KON Games. Today, I’m going to talk about a design I made for an MMO set in the world of Arcanum, Arcanum — Arcanum that I called Arcanum MMO. Hi everyone, it’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk to you about my neverreleased Doctor Who game. Hi everyone, it’s me and Tim. Hi everyone, it’s me, Tim.

item.title: The Outer Worlds Launch Party Video
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to share with you something special: The Outer Worlds launch party video.

Let me explain so, when The Outer Worlds launched, we had a big launch party for it on October 25th 2019. It was awesome.

Um, most of obsidian was there, the development team, the publisher came in from uh private division and some of the The Outer Worlds — um, some of the Take 2 folks. We had a lot of other guests.

item.title: My Rejected Star Trek TNG Script
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about my rejected Star Trek next Generation script.

This is a question from Wes 9353, who asked: have you talked more about the Star Trek TNG episode that you co-wrote, or was that just on the podcast he’s referring to was an interview I did with pistol shrimp game and I’ll put the link below, and that was a fun talk. It was the erquan Masters guys. It was super fun. I encourage you to watch it — about 22 minutes in. I do mention that I co-wrote a script, a screenplay, for Star Trek next Generation. It got rejected, but let me talk to you about it, like how it came to be, what the plot was. And I thought it was a pretty good plot, was a very star tracki in plot.

item.title: My First Day Of College
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to tell you a story about my first day of college.

It’s Friday fun day, so I thought I’d tell this story, and it’s really less about my first day of college and more about my mom, and a lot of people have commented that my mom seemed awesome. My mom was awesome. It’s hard to describe, you know I’m. A lot of people probably think their mom is really good. I just like to tell stories where she’s involved. You can see how cool and fun and, uh, just encouraging she was. So to get this story right, I’ve got to give you some context.

item.title: An "Off The Rails" TTRPG Campaign
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk to you about an off the rails tabletop RPG campaign.

This is me answering a question from Wes 9353, who asked: how far off the rail have you gotten a campaign to go? Now let me clarify: this wasn’t my campaign. Uh, someone else was being the GM in this and but I was a player. There were three players: me, Dave and John. I’m not going to tell you what the module was. It was a professional module purchased with money made by a professional game designer for a professionally produced tabletop role playing game system. All I’m going to tell you was superheroes. Good luck finding out which one.

item.title: My Worst Job Interview
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about my worst job interview.

Now I’ve done videos where I’ve talked about job interviews from the hiring side, about questions I like to ask and tests I like to give. I’ve even talked about questions I’ve received while looking for a job and those videos are linked below. But today I want to talk about the absolute worst experience I ever had looking for a job, and this came out about because I was talking with colleagues in the industry about their worst job interviews and these horrible ones came up about, you know, sexist remarks and really long tests that were work for the project they were interviewing for — just bad questions. And it reminded me that I usually told one job interview story that happened just a few years ago, but I but I realized I had another really awful one that happened near the beginning of my career. So I’m tell you both of them.

item.title: Two Recent Mementos
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about two recent momentos I now have — and they’re behind me, and I’ll Point them out in a few minutes.

This is a Friday fun day so I thought I’d do something fun. I’ve had a lot of people ask about all the momentos that are in these two cabinets — memorabilia from my career days — and I’ve talked about a few of them. I talked about the Fallout foam core board, the really big one.

item.title: Unused GDC 1999 Roadtrip Presentation
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about RPG 2000.

This is a presentation that I was going to give at the 1999 GDC road trip in Austin. Let me give you some context. I was going through my archives and on the inside — the archive of the very first machine I had at troyka — I think I had three different machines — I found a PowerPoint called GDC 99 road trip. I remember making this PowerPoint vaguely, but I did not go to the GDC 99 road trip convention in Austin, which leads me to believe — because, by the way, there were no other notes, no notes on this talk, no notes about the talk, no emails back and forth — that either I made this and never submitted it, or I submitted it or didn’t get in, or maybe even I got in and decided not to go. I have no memory, and what’s weird about this is I would have been making this in early summer of 99, as if I wasn’t busy enough. We were.

item.title: About My Notes, Part 2
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about my notes again.

I’m always mentioning my notes. I’ve done a whole video about my notes, linked below. It’s called about my notes, um, but I see a lot of comments. I shouldn’t say a lot. I see a frequent number of comments where people think, because I tell stories and there are people in them doing not nice things, that I’m still angry or that I’m holding a lot of grudges or that I tend to blame people a lot. And I don’t know what to do about the last one, because I think I’ve said a lot of things where I blame me and the things I’ve done badly. All I can tell you is one: I don’t really feel like there’s any point in staying angry about things and holding grudges, and I really don’t do that and I’m not doing that, despite what it may seem.

item.title: Brushes With Famous People
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about my brushes with famous people.

I — this is, first of all, this is a Friday funay talk — doesn’t have much to do with development, but it occurred while I was a developer, but it’s fun and I I think it’s cool. So maybe you’d like hearing some of the behind the scenes, like celebrity people that I got to meet. Now, some of them I met and talked with some of them. I just saw um and wished I over and talk to um. But looking back on it, I’ve mentioned here and there in videos people I’ve famous people, celebrities that I’ve talked to and I realized, wow, I’ve actually seen and talked to a lot of famous people, uh, over my career. But I think that just happens when you have a really long career and you’re old.

item.title: Snapshots Through My Career
item.description:
Hey everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I wanted to do something fun that I’m going to call snapshots through my career, and

this is kind of this kind of came up because I’ve done a few videos already: one where I showed scenes of a life in game development, which was a lot of pictures that mostly I took during game development — um, except for all the ones of that people took of me at my desk — and I’ve also done a video on data about my games and I’ll link those below, but they’re not very personal. So what I wanted to do was show you me at every stage of my career — I guess I’d call it like every game I made. So what I did is I went through all the photos — I have thousands of them — and I found a photo of me taken during the development of each one of my games. So I’m going to talk about the game and the year it came out and then show a picture of me at that. Age, so I thought it would be fun. It’s nothing huge, so feel free to skip it, but people seem to like the other ones so I thought I’d do this one.

item.title: A College Story
item.description:
Hi everybody. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to tell a story about something that happened to me in college and before I tell the story I want to say the story itself is less important than the reason I want to tell the story which is to me.

It’s one of the stories about something that happened to me that when I tell people the exact same story, I’ve seen people draw wildly different conclusions about the story, including why I’m even telling it. And I’ll tell the story first and then I’ll talk to you about kind of the reactions I got and then a fun um epilogue to the story anyway. So this is a story about when I was in college. In took place my last year, my fourth year n, which would be 1987, and it was the last semester. I was in engineering school and I was enrolled in a class introduction to the Sea language, which some of you are already going.

item.title: Castles (1991) and Stonekeep (1995)
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about castles and stonekeep, two games at interplay.

One came out in ‘95 or ’ 91 and the other came out in ‘95. I have a little bit of code in the second one but I really didn’t have a whole lot to do with it. But Sebastian Ola asked about stories about castles and ston keep and at first I thought I don’t have any, but then I thought you know what I kind of do, especially about ston keep. So if you’re interested in either those games or just what interplay was like in the early and mid 90s, this is a video about stories, because, guess what turns out, I really like talking, stories, if you didn’t, if you didn’t figure that out yet. So let me start with castles. Uh, castles came out in 1991. I did not start at interplay until 1991 and I was only a contractor during that time, which meant I would just come in every couple weeks and drop off.

item.title: Scenes From A Life Of Game Development
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to show you scenes from a life spent in game development.

Let me tell you where this came from. I’m thinking, so do not hold me to this — I’m thinking about writing a book on game development. Basically, get rid of all the Memoir stuff, that gets people riled up and you know why don’t you give me credit, why did you tell the story about me? BL, just stuff about here’s how I recommend making games.

item.title: The Story Of The Wig
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk about the story of the wig.

This is kind of a followup of a video I did last month showing me wearing a bag on my head, and it’s about this photo of me wearing a blonde wig and holding a miniature statue of the Oscars — Oscars. So this is actually in response to: uh, interuser commented: there’s also a picture of you in a blonde wig and, I think, a crown and you’re holding an award or something. Haven’t seen that in a while. Good, um, this photo — I don’t even remember how it got out or where it got posted, but, um, there’s context to it and with, well, with a lot of my stories, there’s a bit of context that leads up to explaining why, I’m wearing a blonde wig and holding a miniature Oscar, and I know it’s silly and I know this doesn’t have much to do with game development, but people like the other story, so I figured, okay, I’ll tell. This one too, besides, you got to have room in your life occasionally for something silly. So the context for this: when I worked at interplay, the producer on my first two games there — bar taale construction set and Rags to Riches — was Thomas R Decker. Tom also came to work at troa on Temple of Elemental Evil as a designer and producer and then as a producer on vampire Bloodlines.

item.title: The Story Of The Bag
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to tell a story about a picture that floats around on the internet of me with a paper bag on my head sitting at my desk.

It’s this picture here, and as with everything, there is a story behind it and I know um people say they like hearing my stories, so this will test that. And also I’m directly answering or replying to l r I n fi loreny who said: for some reason I keep seeing a picture of you sitting at your desk with a paper bag on your head. There must be a fun story behind that one. There is, so let me give you some Contex text for this story. I’ve said — and you can watch the other Fallout videos, this was during Fallout.

item.title: True Stories With Me As The Villain
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk about me being the bad guy.

The reason I want to talk about this is I have seen comments. I read almost all the comments that come in. I see a lot of comments where people say: or a few where people say: wow, Tim really comes across as the hero in all his stories. And it’s not my intent, although I did, you know, I’ve talked to people about this and it’s hard to tell a story from your point of view where you’re the villain. Um, in fact, I would ask people who are saying that women was the last time you publicly talked about being a bad person. But it made me think, because I’ve told several stories already on this channel where I didn’t think I looked good. Um, when I was talking about game developer caution and one of the stories was about a, programmer, who gave me back a really big um for something that I talk thought would take.

item.title: Mementos: My T. Ray Isaac Original
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk about another momento story.

People have asked about stuff in the those uh display cases behind me and I wanted to talk about one of my favorite things, This is me drawn in the style of Fallout of Vault boy by Tay Isaac. This is a Tay Isaac one-of aind original.

I put it in plastic so it doesn’t get damaged. You can see it’s signed by tray. Right there says tray 997.

item.title: The Fallout Team
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk about the Fallout team.

By the way, yes, I know it’s dark, got a busy day pre-dawn, blah, blah. The reason I want to talk about the Fallout team: yeah, my Memoirs. That writing didn’t go well. I’m toying with a making of Fallout writing. Uh, I’ve got about 80- 87 Pages.

If I have my glasses on, I’m going to read from it. But then I was reading through it I just got all the warm fuzzies about the Fallout team. It was such a good team and I want to talk about them as individuals. And then I want to talk about a fun thing that happened that I found um in an old journal entry I wrote, but I put together a list of what I consider to be the core set of personnel who worked on Fallout.

item.title: Apology
item.description: On a previous video that I’ll link below. Someone I’ve worked with in the past was dead named. I’ve privately apologized to Rebecca heinemann, but I wanted to post this short as a retraction and a public apology. I’m sorry, Rebecca.

item.title: Fallout: Nuka Break
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. And today I want to talk about Fallout — Nuka break.

Fallout new break is a fan made Fallout based series — web series that was made by wayside creations.

Let me talk about how I got involved and then what it was like, because this was a really, really neat thing for me to experience, as someone who’s only ever done game development. Being able to watch and even participate a little bit in a Film Production was amazing. So all of it started in October of 2011 in an interview with the game creators Vault, and I’ll link that below. They were interviewing Chris Avalon — he invited me to come because we’re talking about Fallout — and Zach finfrock, who was at Wayside creations and was kind of spearheading the whole, uh, fallout — Nuka break idea. This was an early October 2011. It was right before I actually joined the company, so I was. I drove down from home. It was after I was well, after a month after I left carbine, but I hadn’t started. Obsidian it, in fact after doing this shoot. I think that’s when I talked to them about coming on board.

item.title: Developing South Park: The Stick Of Truth
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about South Park, the Stick of Truth, also lavalier mic, so hopefully this sounds better.

I haven’t talked much about South Park or pillars of Eternity or tyranny, because I wasn’t involved in any of those projects in a lead role, so my viewpoint is mainly as a programmer. I did some design, but mostly I was a programmer, so take these stories as from someone with that somewhat limited Viewpoint. When I talk about a lot of my other games, I was a lead, or even the Project Lead, so I had access to a lot of information of stuff that was going on — not true in South Park.

item.title: My Lost Decade
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about my lost decade which is what I call 1993 to 2003, and I think

this is a pretty appropriate thing to talk about after I’ve talked about my whole work, life, balance, slash, passion, thoughts — and some people know this, some people don’t — but pretty much from the end of making Rags to Riches, which came out in 93, to almost the end of making tempo of Elemental Evil, which came out in 2003, I didn’t do anything except work on video games. Many of you may think, okay, he’s exaggerating. New. I pretty much worked every weekday, probably for about 10 hours until we, probably before the team started crunching, I always felt like I can’t ask people to Crunch, let me try to get some stuff done. So, starting about three to six months before I even think about asking anyone to put an extra time, I was working probably 10 to 12 hours a day and Saturday. So by the time crunch came around, I’d be working 12 to 14 hours a day every day, which meant I’ll talk about what that meant in terms of my life.

item.title: Troika Memories
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I wanted to reminisce about Troika.

I’ve talked about the projects we did there — ironically three of them — Arcanum, Temple of Elemental Evil and vampire bloodlines, and I know when Leonard and I talked, it just sounded like Troika was so incredibly stressful. And it was, and I’m still trying to figure out how to talk about work-life balance versus passion. But I wanted to talk about how fun frika was, and I start off by saying, yes, I’ve worn this shirt before, but that’s because what I wanted to wear from Troika wasn’t a shirt. We were originally wanted to make something besides the shirt. We would talk about pants or shorts that had a Troika logo embroidered on them. We didn’t want to do a hat or a hoodie. I forget where the idea came from, but we had an idea, and even Sharon Shellman, um Jason’s wife and artist on Arcanum and handled our HR and finances, agreed. This was a good idea, so we made foreign boxer shorts.

item.title: Carbine Redux
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk a little more about carbine, because that video generated a lot of comments.

Um, I’m not gonna answer all the questions, but I do want to go over a lot of the topics that came up. Also, I don’t have another carbine shirt, so we’re back to Fallout. First of all, I want to talk about the fact that I tried in that video to stick as closely as I could to events, like things that happened that I documented that happen. Um, I like to stick to events because people have wildly different interpretations of even events. You know, the same thing can happen and everybody interprets the causes of it and who’s to blame and whatever, differently. I mean, can you imagine what it’d be like if I talked about so-and-so being angry or whatever their internal mental or emotional states were? I mean, it would just the interpretation of That’s my interpretation of it and then your interpretation of my. Interpretation, so I try to just stick to on these dates. This person did something, said something, emailed something, I wrote down. But there’s another reason I don’t like to talk about why people did things, and that’s because one I’m not good at it I have no idea half the time why people are doing the things they do. It seems a lot of things they do seem counter to their own, like productivity or the, how good the game is going to be, or even you know them being happy. I I really don’t understand sometimes why people do what they do. But the other reason I don’t like doing it is a lot of people lie about why they’re doing things, and not just to other people. A lot of people lie to themselves while they’re doing something. You know, you see this. A lot of people go look.

item.title: MMO Stories
item.description:
Hey everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about why I got into MMOs, like why I even went to carbine in the first place to make an MMO, because a lot of people thought that this was a bit of a Divergence from what

I had done before, because I made mostly, at least since Fallout, since 94. I’d done nothing but RPGs. Well, I already talked in a previous video how much I really got into LP muds in grad school. I talked about darker Realms, snow cows and stunts. I pulled as a wizard. That, of course, got me into MMOs. When Troika started in 98, that’s when EverQuest came out and EverQuest was pretty much a mud and with Graphics thrown on the front end and I was having so much fun with that game I would go home and play it every night. I had dozens of characters on multiple servers.

item.title: Why I Left Carbine's WildStar
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about why I left carbine Studios.

So I left in 2011, uh, end of June. It’s only the second time I ever walked away — away from a game in development, but it — WildStar, which was that game — did ship. Uh, just like the first game I walked away from ever, Fallout 2 shipped, which makes I mean I’m happy that every game I ever worked on or got paid to work on shipped, even the ones — the two — that I walked away from.

item.title: How I Almost Made Wasteland 2
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about how I almost made Wasteland 2 in the late 90s, something I mentioned when I was having my chat with Scott Campbell kind of mentioned.

Oh yeah, I almost. You know Interplay wasn’t going to get that license and yeah, let me, let me go into detail. So let me give context.

Um, so we talked about how some people on the team had played Wasteland. I haven’t seen of the core team. Probably most of them had played Wasteland, but most of the people who played Wasteland had never finished it. They played it, saw how it worked. It was hard to play those games. Um, early dos games.

item.title: The First Time Fallout Was Almost Canceled
item.description:
Hi everyone. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk to you about the first time Fallout almost got canceled.

Now, what’s interesting about this? I don’t think I’ve ever talked about it publicly, but I don’t even think most people on the team know about this. Most people know about the whole gurp situation and that almost, you know, got the whole project Tangled Up.

Maybe I shouldn’t say cancel, but certainly almost installed, massively changed whatever. I have mentioned that when interplay got the DND license followed almost got canceled because they didn’t see the need to divert, devote resources to this Grade B product when they had DND that they called the license to print money. But there was one other time that it almost got canceled and it was because of me. So let me walk you through a story. So the spring of 95, Scott Everts was looking at open houses in the Orange County area. He decided he wanted to buy some place and he knew I had been.

item.title: Interplay After Hours Game Playing
item.description: Hi everyone, it’s Tim. In previous videos I’ve talked about playing d, and gerps and LP muds. In this video I want to talk about how my game playing really took off when I started interplay. Now I thought I was a game player as a kid, you know in high school and college, but nothing prepared me for just how much game playing went on at interplay after I started. Now this is pretty much after I got hired. So in early 92 to about when Fallout kicked into high gear in 95, which also corresponds to about the time we switched buildings.

item.title: My Time With MUDs (Multi User Dungeons)
item.description:
Hi everyone, it’s me, Tim, while I have a whole list of topics I want to talk about — and the list is getting longer — I spent all day yesterday thinking about muds. So I just jumped it to the top of the queue and I want to talk about muds today.

For those of you who don’t know, mud stands for multi-user Dungeon. It was something that started decades ago, I want to say early 80s. It was a text based multiplayer game, think Zork or any infocom game very similar. You know a description of a room, things you see you can type in text responses like pick up sword or attack monster.

item.title: Making Rags To Riches (1993)
item.description:
Hi. It’s me, Tim. Today I want to talk about Rags to Riches, which is the first game I made at interplay as an employee.

So the, previous game I made there bardstyle construction set. I was a contractor so I did it all at home and I would come in weekly for them to check the progress of what I was doing. But Rags to Riches I got hired as an employee. This was in very early. I think it was January of 92.

item.title: The Fun of The Temple of Elemental Evil
item.description:
Hi everyone, it’s me, Tim, so yesterday’s video about Temple development to evil was a little heavier.

I thought I’d follow it up with a description of how fun it was to make tempo of Elemental Evil so, kind of a Counterpoint to yesterday’s. The first thing I have to say is when I was approached about making a d game, I was ecstatic. I mean, 14 year old d playing Timmy was going. It was just super exciting, especially because I got to pick the module. You know, when I picked tempo of Unto evil — and just so people know that was the part of a grand plan — I was going to make Tempo valento evil. I was going to follow it up because I assumed it would do spectacularly with the D series modules, The Descent Into the Depths, the whole drow series, what it’s called — followed with the penultimate uh q6.

item.title: Pick a topic!
item.description:
Hi, it’s me, Tim.

I’m trying to figure out what topics to talk about first. I’ve got a huge list. I’m not sure which ones to start with, so I thought I’d throw a bunch of them out there and you guys can leave a comment and tell me which one you think I should start with, or which one you find really interesting or you haven’t heard about somewhere else, so we can. I can start before I even got into gaming, or professionally, uh, back when I played d? D in high school as a DM, which is where I learned the most important lesson which drove all the rest of my game development, which is you can make the best story in the world but you can’t make your players actually follow it and you end up just having to go with the flow and inventing consequences for their actions and just moving forward.
